hero.title: plugin-kit-ai
hero.subtitle: Build a plugin once. Ship it to multiple AI agents from one repo. plugin-kit-ai is the home for our first-party plugin lineup and the open-source toolkit behind it.

features.title: One repo instead of scattered setup
features.description: Keep one plugin repo as the source of truth instead of copying the same logic into separate agent-specific folders.

features.title: Start with one strong path, add more later
features.description: Start by building the plugin for the one agent you need right now, then expand to more supported agents when your product actually needs it.

features.title: Validate before handoff
features.description: Use a repeatable generate and validate flow before another teammate, CI job, or end user touches the plugin.

features.title: Honest stable vs beta boundary
features.description: plugin-kit-ai is explicit about what is production-ready, what is public beta, and where you should not promise fake parity yet.

features.title: Clear install and release path
features.description: Homebrew, npm, pipx when published, verified install script, GitHub releases, and docs all point to one clean starting flow.

features.title: Open source from day one
features.description: Use the toolkit today, inspect the source, and follow a transparent workflow while our first-party plugin catalog grows.

plugins.title: Context7
plugins.description: A real multi-agent MCP plugin for fresh library docs and code examples, generated once and shipped to Claude, Codex, Gemini, OpenCode, and Cursor.

plugins.title: GitLab
plugins.description: A real GitLab MCP plugin for projects, issues, merge requests, and DevOps workflows through the official GitLab MCP endpoint.

plugins.title: GitHub
plugins.description: A real GitHub MCP plugin for repository, issues, and pull-request workflows through the official GitHub MCP service with a configured authorization token.

plugins.title: Review Guard
plugins.description: Flag risky tool usage, missing checks, and policy drift before a plugin reaches another teammate or customer.

plugins.title: Release Notes Builder
plugins.description: Turn commits, diffs, and handoff context into clear release notes and changelog drafts for fast publishing.

plugins.title: Docs Sync
plugins.description: Keep README files, docs pages, and support notes aligned with what actually changed in the repo.

plugins.title: Repo Onboarding
plugins.description: Set up contributor guidance, repo instructions, and agent-ready context so new people can start faster.

plugins.title: Command Safety
plugins.description: Block unsafe command patterns, offer safer alternatives, and make approval-sensitive operations easier to review.

plugins.title: Workspace Setup
plugins.description: Prepare agent-specific config, workspace wiring, and shared defaults from one managed source repo.

comparisonRows.feature: One repo for many agent outputs
Add outputs later without starting over

duplicated.note: Every new output starts from scratch

pluginKitAi.note: Docs, releases, and install paths line up

faq.question: What is plugin-kit-ai?
faq.answer: plugin-kit-ai is an open-source toolkit for keeping one plugin repo as the source of truth, then generating and validating the supported agent outputs you actually need.

faq.question: Who should use it?
faq.answer: Use it when you want a real plugin repo, a shared workflow, and a cleaner path than one-off scripts or duplicated agent-specific config.

faq.question: Do I need one repo per agent?
faq.answer: No. The point is to keep one authored source repo, then target supported outputs for Claude, Codex, Gemini, OpenCode, Cursor, and others from that same base instead of rebuilding each one from scratch.

faq.question: Is every supported output equally stable today?
faq.answer: No. Some lanes are production-ready, and some remain public beta. plugin-kit-ai is explicit about that boundary so you can make honest product promises.

faq.question: What is the fastest way to start?
faq.answer: Install plugin-kit-ai, create a repo with init, then run render and validate --strict. Most teams should start with the Quickstart. If your team is intentionally Python-first, go straight to the Python guide.

faq.question: Which path should I start with: Go, Python, or Node?
faq.answer: Start with Go when you want the strongest default path. Choose Node/TypeScript for the main supported non-Go path. Choose Python when the repo stays local and your team is already Python-first.

download.title: Get started
download.note: Pick an install path, open the docs, and start from one clean plugin repo.

installChannels.title: Homebrew
installChannels.description: Recommended local install for machines that already use Homebrew.
installChannels.note: Best default when Homebrew is available.

installChannels.title: npm
installChannels.description: Official JavaScript ecosystem wrapper for teams that already center their workflow on npm.
installChannels.note: Public-beta wrapper that downloads the matching verified release binary.

installChannels.title: pipx
installChannels.description: Python ecosystem wrapper when the corresponding release is published to PyPI.
installChannels.note: Public-beta wrapper. Use this channel only when the target release is available on PyPI.

installChannels.title: Verified install script
installChannels.description: Fallback bootstrap path that resolves the latest published stable release and verifies checksums.
installChannels.note: Good when package-manager setup is not what you want first.

installChannels.title: GitHub Releases
installChannels.description: Browse published release notes, artifacts, and version history directly.
installChannels.note: Use this when you want the raw release surface.

installChannels.title: Docs quickstart
installChannels.description: Jump straight into the quickstart and the broader docs site.
installChannels.note: Best next step once the CLI is installed.

quickstartSteps.title: Install the CLI
quickstartSteps.note: Homebrew is the recommended local install path for the CLI itself.

quickstartSteps.title: Create the strongest default repo first
quickstartSteps.note: The default path starts with the strongest supported production story.

quickstartSteps.title: Generate managed target files
quickstartSteps.note: Generate the managed target artifacts from one authored repo.

quickstartSteps.title: Validate before handoff
quickstartSteps.note: Use the strict validation gate before CI, teammate handoff, or release.

supportLanes.name: Claude plugin package
supportLanes.note: Production-ready for the stable default hook subset. Settings and manifest support are solid; some wider Claude package surfaces still stay beta.

supportLanes.name: Codex runtime and package lanes
supportLanes.note: The Codex notify runtime lane and the official Codex package lane are both production-ready today.

supportLanes.name: Gemini extension lane
supportLanes.note: Gemini extension packaging is production-ready, and the Go runtime lane is production-ready for the current stable 9-hook contract.

supportLanes.name: OpenCode workspace-config lane
supportLanes.note: Repo-local config, MCP, skills, commands, agents, themes, and plugin refs are supported, but this is still a workspace-config target, not a production-ready runtime lane.

supportLanes.name: Cursor workspace-config lane
supportLanes.note: Project rules and MCP config are covered, while root CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md stay reserved for plugin boundary docs; Cursor remains a strict workspace-config lane.
